summary: I was born and raised in Greenville, North Carolina, spending my first eighteen years there. Back then, Greenville was a town of about 21,000 people. My family lived in town, and my parents worked as laborers in various jobs. Growing up in Greenville shaped much of my early experience, including my interactions with the local community and the segregated environment I lived in.

I attended C.M. Eppes High School, which was the all-black high school in Greenville during segregation. This was in the 1960s, as I am about to turn sixty now. The school was part of the segregated system we grew up in, and it was where I first became aware of some of the social dynamics around me, including the presence of openly gay individuals like Lester. My time there was formative, both academically and socially.

Growing up African American in Greenville meant living in a segregated community but also one where people were just part of the fabric of life. The African American community I knew was not as homophobic as some might think, especially before desegregation. People who were gay were just part of the community, not ostracized, and many were accepted for their talents and contributions. Over time, especially after desegregation, some attitudes shifted, possibly influenced by wider cultural and religious factors, but I still found strong bonds and acceptance within the African American community.

I grew up during segregation in the United States, specifically in North Carolina, where the schools and communities were separated by race. This was the norm in my early years, and it shaped much of my daily life and interactions. Segregation was something you didn’t always think about until you encountered white people or crossed those boundaries. It wasn’t until later, as I got older and went to high school, that I started to reflect more on what segregation really meant and how it affected us all.
